Example 7
Consider the path in R2 given by ⃗r(t) = cos(t)⃗i + sin(t) ⃗j.
 1. Show that the path is a circle.
 2. Prove that the velocity vector is always tangential to the
    circle.
 3. Prove that the acceleration of the particle is directed to
    the centre of the circle. (This is always the case for
    uniform circular motion.)

 Vector fields

                                                                                                                                                                                     →
                                                                                                                                                                                     −
    I A 2D vector field is a function f⃗ : R2 → R2 , that is, a                                                                 I Another example of a vector field is the gradient ∇ϕ of a
      function that assigns a 2D vector f⃗(x, y) to every                                                                         scalar field ϕ, which tells us the direction and magnitude
      point (x, y) in the plane.                                                                                                  of maximum increase of the scalar field ϕ. It is important
                                                                                                                                  to remember that we think of each vector in a vector field
    I Similarly, a 3D vector field f⃗ : R3 → R3 assigns a 3D                                                                      as assigned to a particular point in space and not with its
      vector f⃗(x, y, z) to each point (x, y, z) in space.                                                                        base at the origin.
    I An example of a 3D vector field is the air velocity in a                                                                  I Vector fields provide the natural mathematical tool for
      room. We could imagine measuring the air velocity (speed                                                                    studying all kinds of fluid (gas or liquid) flows.
      and direction) at every point in space. There would be
      large vectors near the AC ducts and small vectors in the
      corners of the room where there is little air movement.

Example 13 (Inverse square law of gravitation)                                                                                   Gradient, Divergence, and Curl
Consider a particle of mass M located at a point P0 , and a
second particle of mass m located at a point P . According to
Newton, the gravitational force F⃗G on m at P due to M at P0
has magnitude                                                                                                                      Earlier, we introduced the vector differentiation operator, del
                                 GM m                                                                                              or nabla,
                        ∥F⃗G ∥ =         ,                                                                                                            →
                                                                                                                                                      −     ∂ ⃗     ∂ ⃗     ∂ ⃗
                                    r2                                                                                                                ∇=       i+     j+       k,
                                                                                                                                                            ∂x     ∂y       ∂z
where G ≈ 6.67430 × 10−11 m3 kg−1 s−2 is the gravitational
constant and r is the distance between P and P0 .                                                                                  and defined the gradient of a scalar field ϕ to be the vector
The gravitational force F⃗G is a vector field, given by                                                                            field
                                                                                                                                                         −
                                                                                                                                                         →       ∂ϕ ⃗ ∂ϕ ⃗ ∂ϕ ⃗
                                                                                                                                               grad ϕ = ∇ϕ =        i+       j+      k.
                                ⃗r  GM m                                                                                                                         ∂x      ∂y       ∂z
                   F⃗G = −∥F⃗G ∥ = − 3 ⃗r,
                                 r   r
           −−→
where ⃗r = P0 P . The minus sign indicates that F⃗G is directed
from P to P0 , that is, gravity is an attractive force.
